Pandowdy Apple IIe Emulator
Table of Contents

Class CPUAdapter

Namespace
Pandowdy.EmuCore
Assembly
Pandowdy.EmuCore.dll

Adapter wrapping a third-party 6502 CPU emulator for Pandowdy compatibility (stopgap implementation).

public class CPUAdapter : ICpu
Inheritance
object
CPUAdapter
Implements
ICpu
Inherited Members
object.Equals(object)
object.Equals(object, object)
object.GetHashCode()
object.GetType()
object.MemberwiseClone()
object.ReferenceEquals(object, object)

Remarks

⚠️ TEMPORARY IMPLEMENTATION: This adapter wraps the legacy Emulator.CPU class from the 6502.NET project to provide compatibility with Pandowdy's ICpu interface. It will be replaced by a native 6502 implementation in a future update.

Purpose: Bridges the gap between the legacy CPU emulator and Pandowdy's architecture. The legacy CPU requires explicit connection to a bus before each operation, while Pandowdy's design passes the bus as a parameter. This adapter handles the connection/disconnection pattern.

Connection Pattern: The legacy CPU uses a "Connect-Execute-Disconnect" pattern for each bus operation. The bus connection is temporary and scoped to the operation lifetime: 

_oldCpu.Connect(bus);    // Connect to bus
_oldCpu.Clock();         // Execute operation
_oldCpu.Connect(null);   // Disconnect
This pattern is repeated for every bus operation (Clock, Reset, interrupts), adding overhead compared to a native implementation that would hold a bus reference.

Thread Safety: Thread safety is guaranteed by VA2M's command queue architecture. All CPU operations are serialized on the emulator thread and executed at instruction boundaries via ProcessPending(). This ensures the Connect-Disconnect pattern cannot be interrupted by operations from other threads, eliminating race conditions.

Performance Considerations: The connection overhead is minimal but not ideal. Read/Write operations are marked with [MethodImpl(MethodImplOptions.AggressiveInlining)] to reduce call overhead since they're invoked frequently during instruction execution.

Dependency: Requires the Emulator project from legacy/6502.NET, which is a third-party 6502 emulator. The replacement will eliminate this external dependency.

Constructors

CPUAdapter(CPU)

Initializes a new instance of the CPUAdapter class.

public CPUAdapter(CPU cpu)

Parameters

cpu CPU

The legacy Emulator.CPU instance to wrap. This CPU instance should be pre-configured but not yet connected to a bus.

Remarks

The adapter takes ownership of the CPU instance and manages its connection lifecycle. The CPU should not be used directly after being wrapped by this adapter.

Exceptions

ArgumentNullException

Thrown if cpu is null.

Properties

A

Gets the Accumulator register, the primary register for arithmetic and logic operations.

public byte A { get; }

Property Value

byte

An 8-bit value used as the primary operand for ALU operations, loads, stores, and comparisons. Most 6502 instructions operate on or with the accumulator.

Remarks

Directly exposes the Accumulator from the wrapped CPU. No adaptation needed.

PC

Gets the Program Counter register, which points to the next instruction to execute.

public ushort PC { get; }

Property Value

ushort

A 16-bit address ($0000-$FFFF) indicating the memory location of the next instruction byte to be fetched and executed.

Remarks

Directly exposes the Program Counter from the wrapped CPU. No adaptation needed.

SP

Gets the Stack Pointer register, which points to the current top of the stack.

public byte SP { get; }

Property Value

byte

An 8-bit offset ($00-$FF) within the stack page ($0100-$01FF). The actual stack address is $0100 + SP. The stack grows downward (SP decrements on push).

Remarks

Directly exposes the Stack Pointer from the wrapped CPU. No adaptation needed.

Status

Gets the Processor Status register containing condition flags.

public ProcessorStatus Status { get; }

Property Value

ProcessorStatus

An 8-bit register containing seven status flags:

  • Bit 7 (N): Negative flag - Set if result is negative (bit 7 = 1)
  • Bit 6 (V): Overflow flag - Set if signed arithmetic overflow occurred
  • Bit 5: Unused (always 1)
  • Bit 4 (B): Break flag - Set if interrupt was caused by BRK instruction
  • Bit 3 (D): Decimal mode flag - Enables BCD arithmetic (not used on Apple II)
  • Bit 2 (I): Interrupt disable flag - When set, IRQ interrupts are ignored
  • Bit 1 (Z): Zero flag - Set if result is zero
  • Bit 0 (C): Carry flag - Set by arithmetic operations and shifts

Remarks

Directly exposes the Processor Status register from the wrapped CPU. No adaptation needed.

X

Gets the X Index register, used for indexed addressing modes and counters.

public byte X { get; }

Property Value

byte

An 8-bit value commonly used for indexed addressing (e.g., "LDA $1000,X"), loop counters, and temporary storage.

Remarks

Directly exposes the X Index register from the wrapped CPU. No adaptation needed.

Y

Gets the Y Index register, used for indexed addressing modes and counters.

public byte Y { get; }

Property Value

byte

An 8-bit value commonly used for indexed addressing (e.g., "LDA $1000,Y"), loop counters, and temporary storage. Similar to X but with fewer addressing modes.

Remarks

Directly exposes the Y Index register from the wrapped CPU. No adaptation needed.

Methods

Clock(IAppleIIBus)

Executes one clock cycle of the CPU.

public void Clock(IAppleIIBus bus)

Parameters

bus IAppleIIBus

The system bus for memory and I/O access during this cycle.

Remarks

Cycle Execution: The 6502 is a multi-cycle processor. Each call to this method advances the CPU by one cycle:

  • Cycle 1: Fetch instruction opcode
  • Cycles 2-6: Fetch operands, calculate addresses, execute operation (varies by instruction)
  • Final cycle: Write result if needed

Timing: Call this method at approximately 1.023 MHz for accurate Apple IIe timing (1,020,484 Hz to be precise). The emulator typically runs this in a loop, either continuously or in bursts synchronized with video refresh.

Connection Pattern: Uses the Connect-Execute-Disconnect pattern required by the legacy CPU. This is the most frequently called method in the emulator, so the connection overhead is most noticeable here. The native replacement will eliminate this overhead.

Thread Safety: Thread safety is guaranteed by VA2M's architecture. This method is only ever called from the emulator thread (via Bus.Clock() in VA2M.RunAsync() or VA2M.Clock()). All external operations are queued and executed at instruction boundaries via ProcessPending(), ensuring serial execution with no race conditions.

InterruptRequest(IAppleIIBus)

Triggers an Interrupt Request (IRQ), causing the CPU to jump to the IRQ handler.

public void InterruptRequest(IAppleIIBus bus)

Parameters

bus IAppleIIBus

The system bus for pushing return address and reading IRQ vector.

Remarks

IRQ Process: When triggered (and not masked by the I flag):

  1. Connects to the bus temporarily
  2. Finishes current instruction
  3. Pushes PC and Status to stack
  4. Reads IRQ vector from $FFFE/$FFFF
  5. Sets PC to vector address
  6. Sets I flag (disable further IRQs)
  7. Disconnects from the bus

Apple IIe Usage: Used for peripheral interrupts, keyboard input, and timer-based events. The handler must eventually execute RTI (Return from Interrupt) to restore CPU state and clear the I flag.

Connection Pattern: Uses the Connect-Execute-Disconnect pattern required by the legacy CPU.

Thread Safety: Thread safety is guaranteed by VA2M's command queue, which ensures this method executes serially on the emulator thread at instruction boundaries.

IsInstructionComplete()

Checks if the current instruction has completed execution.

public bool IsInstructionComplete()

Returns

bool

true if the current instruction has finished all its cycles; false if the instruction is still in progress.

Remarks

Multi-Cycle Instructions: 6502 instructions take 2-7 cycles to complete. This method allows the emulator to determine when an instruction boundary has been reached, which is useful for:

  • Debugging (stepping through instructions)
  • Breakpoints (stopping at instruction boundaries)
  • Timing-sensitive operations (syncing with video refresh)

Direct Delegation: Simply forwards to the wrapped CPU's method.

NonMaskableInterrupt(IAppleIIBus)

Triggers a Non-Maskable Interrupt (NMI), unconditionally jumping to the NMI handler.

public void NonMaskableInterrupt(IAppleIIBus bus)

Parameters

bus IAppleIIBus

The system bus for pushing return address and reading NMI vector.

Remarks

NMI Process: Always executed (cannot be masked):

  1. Connects to the bus temporarily
  2. Finishes current instruction
  3. Pushes PC and Status to stack
  4. Reads NMI vector from $FFFA/$FFFB
  5. Sets PC to vector address
  6. Sets I flag (prevent IRQ during NMI)
  7. Disconnects from the bus

Apple IIe Usage: NMI is rarely used on the Apple IIe. Some expansion cards may use it for critical events that must be handled immediately regardless of the interrupt disable flag state.

Connection Pattern: Uses the Connect-Execute-Disconnect pattern required by the legacy CPU.

Thread Safety: Thread safety is guaranteed by VA2M's command queue, which ensures this method executes serially on the emulator thread at instruction boundaries.

Read(ushort)

Reads a byte from memory at the specified address.

public byte Read(ushort address)

Parameters

address ushort

16-bit memory address to read from ($0000-$FFFF).

Returns

byte

The byte value at the specified address.

Remarks

Performance: Marked with [AggressiveInlining] because this method is called frequently during instruction execution (multiple times per instruction for multi-byte operands and addressing mode calculations). Inlining eliminates call overhead.

Direct Delegation: Simply forwards to the wrapped CPU's Read method. The actual bus access is handled by the legacy CPU implementation.

Reset(IAppleIIBus)

Resets the CPU to its power-on state.

public void Reset(IAppleIIBus bus)

Parameters

bus IAppleIIBus

The system bus for reading the reset vector.

Remarks

Reset Sequence: The 6502 reset process:

  1. Connects to the bus temporarily
  2. Reads the reset vector from $FFFC/$FFFD
  3. Sets PC to the vector address
  4. Initializes SP, Status register, and other state
  5. Disconnects from the bus

Connection Pattern: Uses the Connect-Execute-Disconnect pattern required by the legacy CPU. The bus connection is temporary and released after reset.

Thread Safety: Thread safety is guaranteed by VA2M's command queue, which ensures this method executes serially on the emulator thread at instruction boundaries. No race conditions can occur.

ToString()

Returns a string representation of the CPU state.

public override string ToString()

Returns

string

A string showing current register values and processor status, typically formatted for debugging and logging purposes.

Remarks

Delegates to the wrapped CPU's ToString implementation, which typically includes PC, SP, A, X, Y, and Status register values in a readable format.

Write(ushort, byte)

Writes a byte to memory at the specified address.

public void Write(ushort address, byte data)

Parameters

address ushort

16-bit memory address to write to ($0000-$FFFF).

data byte

Byte value to write to the specified address.

Remarks

Performance: Marked with [AggressiveInlining] because this method is called frequently during instruction execution (for store operations, stack pushes, and memory-modifying instructions). Inlining eliminates call overhead.

Direct Delegation: Simply forwards to the wrapped CPU's Write method. The actual bus access is handled by the legacy CPU implementation.
